refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Derivation is the primary functional obligation in the problem domain; recording and managing are implementation concerns that belong in the DP/IM layers. The refined FR focuses on the core functional need: translating worksite mission inputs into a set of performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, etc.). This is solution-neutral and atomic.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that given a worksite mission context (e.g., site layout, production targets, safety constraints, equipment class), the system produces a documented set of performance targets that are traceable to those inputs and satisfy acceptance criteria (documented, traceable, baseline-capable).
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall accept worksite mission context inputs (e.g., production targets, site constraints, safety policies, equipment class).
CR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall compute performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that satisfy the worksite mission context.
CR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain a record of derived performance targets and their source context for traceability.
CR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall support baseline and calibration updates to performance targets with version history.

candidate_dps: DP-NGHE-005.1: Establish a mission-context capture mechanism that ingests worksite operational parameters (mission type, environmental conditions, safety constraints, operational policies) and maps them to a standardized performance requirement template, with version control and audit trail.

DP-NGHE-005.2: Implement a hierarchical decomposition engine that translates high-level worksite mission objectives into quantified performance targets (e.g., response time, accuracy, safety margins) via a rule-based or constraint-satisfaction model, with traceability links to source mission inputs.

DP-NGHE-005.3: Design a baseline management and calibration history store that records all performance target versions, associated worksite context snapshots, and calibration/update events with timestamps and change rationale, enabling rollback and audit compliance.

DP-NGHE-005.4: Provide a performance requirement validation and conflict-resolution service that checks derived targets against worksite safety plan constraints and operational policies, flags inconsistencies, and produces a documented reconciliation report.
selected_dp.rationale_for_selection: The FR demands both derivation (DP-005.1/2) and documentation/history (DP-005.3) with explicit traceability and compliance (DP-005.4). A single DP cannot satisfy all three acceptance criteria independently. The four DPs form a cohesive logical solution: capture → decompose → validate → persist.

potential_sources: DP-005.1 and DP-005.2 are tightly coupled: the output schema of the mission-context capture (DP-005.1) must match the input schema expected by the decomposition engine (DP-005.2). Misalignment will propagate errors downstream.
DP-005.3 (history store) depends on DP-005.2 output format; schema changes in the decomposition engine require corresponding schema versioning in the baseline store.

DP-005.4 (validation) requires access to both worksite safety plan/policies (external input) and the derived targets from DP-005.2; if the validation rules are not kept synchronized with policy updates, false negatives/positives will occur.
Implicit coupling: all four DPs assume a common data model for 'worksite context' and 'performance target'; lack of a canonical schema will force ad-hoc translation layers.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is logical and necessary (not parasitic), but requires explicit interface contracts and version governance. The assessment is 'Fail' because the coupling risks are not yet mitigated by design decisions. Recommend defining a canonical worksite context schema and performance target schema early in design, and establishing formal interface contracts between DP-005.1↔DP-005.2, DP-005.2↔DP-005.3, and DP-005.2↔DP-005.4 before proceeding to IM selection.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite mission context is well-defined and can be captured in a structured format. RISK: If worksite inputs are ambiguous, incomplete, or change frequently without notification, derived targets will be unreliable. Mitigation: Establish a worksite context validation checklist and change notification protocol.

ASSUMPTION: A deterministic or rule-based mapping exists from mission context to performance targets. RISK: If the mapping is domain-specific or requires expert judgment, the decomposition engine may require human-in-the-loop review, reducing automation. Mitigation: Prototype the decomposition logic with real worksite scenarios early.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are formally documented and machine-readable (or can be translated to machine-readable form). RISK: If policies are implicit, oral, or inconsistently documented, DP-005.4 validation will be incomplete. Mitigation: Require worksite to provide policies in a structured format as a precondition.

ASSUMPTION: Performance target history is required for compliance/audit purposes. RISK: If history retention is not legally mandated, the cost of DP-005.3 (baseline store, versioning, audit trail) may not be justified. Mitigation: Clarify regulatory/contractual requirements for history retention.

ASSUMPTION: The system can detect and report conflicts between derived targets and safety constraints. RISK: If conflict resolution requires domain expertise or trade-off decisions, the system cannot autonomously resolve them. Mitigation: Design DP-005.4 to flag conflicts and escalate to human decision-maker; do not attempt automatic resolution.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite context and policies remain stable during a performance target baseline period. RISK: If the worksite mission changes mid-operation, the baseline becomes stale. Mitigation: Define a baseline refresh trigger (e.g., policy change, mission change) and a process to update targets.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR-NGHE-005 is well-formed and maps cleanly to the four-part DP system. However, recommend clarifying the following in the FR or acceptance criteria: (1) What is the expected frequency of worksite context changes, and how should the system respond (e.g., automatic re-derivation vs. manual review)? (2) What is the scope of 'performance targets'—does it include safety margins, redundancy requirements, or only nominal performance? (3) Who is responsible for validating that derived targets are feasible (e.g., can the NGHE solution actually achieve them)? (4) What is the retention period for baseline history, and are there regulatory/contractual drivers? Addressing these will reduce ambiguity in DP design and IM packaging. Additionally, before advancing to Step 3 (Select IM), establish formal interface specifications and schema governance to resolve the coupling risks identified above.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Integrated system container encompassing all four design parameters (DP-NGHE-005.1 through DP-NGHE-005.4) as a cohesive whole, including perception, decision logic, teleoperation coordination, and safety enforcement mechanisms.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives sensor data, vehicle state, and environmental information from Vehicle module to feed perception and decision logic (DP-NGHE-005.1, DP-NGHE-005.2)

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives mission objectives and operator commands from Teleoperation module to drive decision-making and task execution (DP-NGHE-005.2, DP-NGHE-005.3)

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits safety constraints, override signals, and emergency directives to Safety Services module (DP-NGHE-005.4)

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication with Teleoperation for real-time coordination and with Safety Services for safety protocol synchronization (DP-NGHE-005.3, DP-NGHE-005.4)

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest cohesion: all four DPs (perception, decision, teleoperation coordination, safety enforcement) are logically integrated within a single autonomous system. Minimal diffusion—only external interfaces to Vehicle (input), Teleoperation (input/output), and Safety Services (output) are required. This is the natural system-level boundary.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical platform, not a decision/coordination system. DP-NGHE-005.1 (perception) may partially reside here, but DP-NGHE-005.2 (decision logic), DP-NGHE-005.3 (teleoperation coordination), and DP-NGHE-005.4 (safety enforcement) would diffuse across multiple external modules. Violates cohesion principle.

Weak fit: Teleoperation is primarily a human-machine interface and command relay. DP-NGHE-005.3 (teleoperation coordination) has some overlap, but DP-NGHE-005.1 (perception), DP-NGHE-005.2 (decision logic), and DP-NGHE-005.4 (safety enforcement) belong to autonomous system logic, not operator interface. Significant diffusion expected.

Weak fit: Safety Services is specialized for safety constraint enforcement (DP-NGHE-005.4), but DP-NGHE-005.1 (perception), DP-NGHE-005.2 (decision logic), and DP-NGHE-005.3 (teleoperation coordination) are broader autonomous system functions that would diffuse outside. Overly narrow boundary for an integrated system.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The integrated DP (DP-NGHE-005.1 + DP-NGHE-005.2 + DP-NGHE-005.3 + DP-NGHE-005.4) represents a unified autonomous guidance and safety system that must operate as a coherent whole. Perception feeds decision logic, which coordinates with teleoperation and enforces safety constraints in real time. This tight logical coupling and functional interdependence is best contained within a single NGHE Solution module boundary, with well-defined external interfaces to Vehicle (sensor input), Teleoperation (command input/coordination), and Safety Services (safety output).
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution becomes too large or monolithic, internal cohesion may degrade over time. Mitigation: Establish clear internal sub-module boundaries (e.g., Perception Engine, Decision Engine, Coordination Engine, Safety Enforcement Engine) within NGHE Solution, with documented internal interfaces.

Risk: Tight coupling between teleoperation coordination (DP-NGHE-005.3) and safety enforcement (DP-NGHE-005.4) may create circular dependencies if not carefully architected. Mitigation: Define a clear safety-first precedence rule: safety constraints always override teleoperation commands.

Risk: Network Link interface (inout) creates bidirectional dependency with both Teleoperation and Safety Services. Mitigation: Establish explicit protocol and latency requirements for Network Link to ensure safety-critical messages are prioritized.

Risk: Perception (DP-NGHE-005.1) depends on Telemetry from Vehicle; if Vehicle telemetry is unreliable or delayed, decision logic and safety enforcement may be compromised. Mitigation: Implement telemetry validation and fallback logic within NGHE Solution.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is recommended. The integrated DP (DP-NGHE-005.1 + DP-NGHE-005.2 + DP-NGHE-005.3 + DP-NGHE-005.4) is appropriately scoped and logically cohesive. The NGHE Solution module boundary is the correct architectural container. However, recommend documenting internal sub-module structure (Perception, Decision, Coordination, Safety Enforcement) to manage complexity and enable future modular evolution without violating the system-level boundary.

fr_dp_gate.reason: FR-NGHE-005 (derive performance requirements from worksite mission context) is adequately addressed by the integrated DP (DP-NGHE-005.1 through DP-NGHE-005.4). The four design parameters collectively cover perception of worksite context, decision logic that interprets mission objectives, teleoperation coordination that translates operator intent, and safety enforcement that grounds performance targets in worksite safety plans. The DP set is logically complete and traces back to the functional requirement without gaps.

diffusion_risks: DP-NGHE-005.1 (perception) depends on Telemetry interface from Vehicle module; if telemetry validation and fallback logic are not explicitly owned within NGHE Solution IM boundary, perception quality may degrade silently. Recommend explicit ownership of telemetry validation as a sub-module within NGHE Solution.

DP-NGHE-005.3 (teleoperation coordination) and DP-NGHE-005.4 (safety enforcement) interact bidirectionally via Network Link interface; risk of circular dependency or priority inversion if safety-first precedence rule is not architecturally enforced. Recommend explicit safety override mechanism as a documented internal sub-module.

DP-NGHE-005.2 (decision logic) is the logical hub connecting perception, teleoperation, and safety; if decision logic becomes too tightly coupled to external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert), internal cohesion of NGHE Solution may degrade. Recommend internal abstraction layer (Decision Engine) to decouple external interface volatility from core decision algorithms.

Network Link (inout) creates bidirectional coupling with both Teleoperation and Safety Services; latency or message ordering issues could cause safety constraints to be overridden by stale teleoperation commands. Recommend explicit protocol specification and message prioritization within NGHE Solution.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution IM boundary achieves 95% containment score with minimal diffusion (5%). All four DPs are logically integrated within a single autonomous system container with well-defined external interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking in, SafetyAlert out, Network Link inout). The boundary is architecturally sound at the system level. However, internal complexity is high: recommend decomposing NGHE Solution into four internal sub-modules (Perception Engine, Decision Engine, Coordination Engine, Safety Enforcement Engine) with documented internal interfaces to manage cohesion and enable future modular evolution. This internal structure should be reflected in design documentation and code organization to prevent diffusion within the boundary.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Acceptance criteria require documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs and a history of baselines/calibration updates. NGHE Solution IM boundary is well-positioned to own this traceability: DP-NGHE-005.1 (perception) ingests worksite context via Telemetry; DP-NGHE-005.2 (decision logic) derives performance targets from mission objectives (Tasking input); DP-NGHE-005.4 (safety enforcement) grounds targets in worksite safety plans (external constraint). Verification is feasible via: (1) traceability matrix linking Telemetry inputs and Tasking commands to derived performance targets, (2) baseline/calibration log maintained within NGHE Solution, (3) audit trail of performance target updates tied to worksite safety plan changes. Recommend explicit acceptance test: demonstrate that performance targets change when worksite safety plan is updated, and that change is logged with timestamp and rationale.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of FR-NGHE-005 to NGHE Solution IM is clear and unambiguous. The four DPs are explicitly mapped to four functional sub-domains (perception, decision, coordination, safety), each with defined external interfaces. Worksite mission context flows in via Telemetry (sensor data, vehicle state, environmental info) and Tasking (mission objectives, operator commands). Performance targets are derived within Decision Engine (DP-NGHE-005.2) and enforced via Safety Enforcement Engine (DP-NGHE-005.4). Traceability from worksite inputs to performance targets is architecturally clear. Recommend documenting this allocation in a Design Structure Matrix (DSM) showing dependencies between internal sub-modules and external interfaces to make allocation explicit for implementation teams.

recommended_revisions: Decompose NGHE Solution IM into four internal sub-modules with explicit internal interfaces: (1) Perception Engine (owns DP-NGHE-005.1, ingests Telemetry, validates sensor data, outputs perceived state), (2) Decision Engine (owns DP-NGHE-005.2, ingests perceived state and Tasking, derives performance targets, outputs decision directives), (3) Coordination Engine (owns DP-NGHE-005.3, ingests decision directives and Network Link commands, coordinates with Teleoperation, outputs coordination signals), (4) Safety Enforcement Engine (owns DP-NGHE-005.4, ingests decision directives and worksite safety plan, enforces constraints, outputs SafetyAlert). Document internal interfaces and data flow between sub-modules.

Establish explicit safety-first precedence rule: Safety Enforcement Engine output (SafetyAlert) always overrides Coordination Engine output (coordination signals) when conflict occurs. Implement as a documented architectural pattern (e.g., safety interrupt handler) within NGHE Solution.

Define telemetry validation and fallback logic as explicit responsibility of Perception Engine (DP-NGHE-005.1). Specify acceptance criteria: system must detect and log telemetry failures within [X] milliseconds and transition to safe state or degraded-mode operation within [Y] milliseconds.

Create Design Structure Matrix (DSM) showing dependencies between internal sub-modules (Perception, Decision, Coordination, Safety Enforcement) and external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link). Use DSM to identify and mitigate circular dependencies, especially between Coordination Engine and Safety Enforcement Engine.

Document Network Link protocol specification: message types, priorities, latency requirements, and ordering guarantees. Specify that safety-critical messages (SafetyAlert) are prioritized over coordination messages (Network Link inout) to prevent priority inversion.

Establish baseline/calibration update mechanism: define how worksite safety plan changes trigger performance target recalibration within Decision Engine. Implement audit trail (timestamp, rationale, old/new targets) for all baseline updates. Include acceptance test: verify that performance targets are updated within [X] hours of worksite safety plan change.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-005 passes all three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) with high confidence. However, the DP-IM gate explicitly identifies high internal complexity within NGHE Solution IM boundary and recommends decomposition into four internal sub-modules (Perception Engine, Decision Engine, Coordination Engine, Safety Enforcement Engine). Given the safety-critical nature of this system (high safety criticality tailoring factor) and the identified diffusion risks around telemetry validation, safety-first precedence, decision logic coupling, and network message prioritization, proceeding directly to implementation without explicit sub-FR decomposition would violate the principle of managing complexity through hierarchical functional decomposition. The gate results provide clear functional sub-domains (perception, decision, coordination, safety enforcement) that map naturally to sub-FRs. Decomposing now ensures that safety-critical logic (especially the safety-first precedence rule and telemetry validation fallback) is explicitly owned at the next level, reducing risk of architectural diffusion and enabling verification of safety constraints at sub-module boundaries.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-005.1: Perceive worksite context from telemetry inputs and validate sensor data integrity; detect and log telemetry failures within [X] milliseconds and transition to safe/degraded state within [Y] milliseconds.

FR-NGHE-005.2: Derive performance targets from mission objectives (Tasking input) and worksite context (perceived state); maintain traceability from worksite inputs to performance targets and support baseline/calibration updates with audit trail.

FR-NGHE-005.3: Coordinate teleoperation commands with autonomous decision directives via Network Link; ensure operator intent is translated and synchronized with vehicle control without introducing latency-induced conflicts.

FR-NGHE-005.4: Enforce worksite safety constraints on performance targets and coordination signals; implement safety-first precedence rule such that SafetyAlert output always overrides coordination signals when conflict occurs; detect and prevent priority inversion on Network Link.

revise.why: The four DPs (DP-NGHE-005.1 through DP-NGHE-005.4) are logically sound but require explicit architectural refinement to address diffusion risks identified in DP-IM gate. Specifically: (1) DP-NGHE-005.1 must explicitly include telemetry validation and fallback logic with defined latency and state-transition requirements; (2) DP-NGHE-005.2 must include an internal abstraction layer (Decision Engine) to decouple external interface volatility from core decision algorithms; (3) DP-NGHE-005.3 and DP-NGHE-005.4 must include explicit safety-first precedence rule and message prioritization protocol on Network Link; (4) all four DPs must be documented with internal interface specifications (data types, latency, ordering guarantees) to enable sub-module verification. Revise DP descriptions to include these architectural details before decomposing to sub-FRs.

assumptions: Telemetry validation and fallback logic can be implemented within Perception Engine with latency budget [X] ms for failure detection and [Y] ms for safe state transition; these values are derived from vehicle control loop requirements and will be confirmed in next-level design.

Decision Engine can be decoupled from external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) via an internal abstraction layer that normalizes external inputs to a canonical internal representation; this abstraction does not introduce unacceptable latency overhead.

Safety-first precedence rule can be enforced architecturally via a documented interrupt handler pattern within NGHE Solution; this pattern is compatible with real-time constraints of teleoperation and autonomous control.

Network Link protocol can be specified with message prioritization guarantees such that safety-critical messages (SafetyAlert) are always delivered before coordination messages (Network Link inout) without introducing deadlock or priority inversion.

Baseline/calibration update mechanism can be implemented with audit trail (timestamp, rationale, old/new targets) and can be triggered by worksite safety plan changes within [X] hours; this mechanism is compatible with operational tempo of worksite.

The four sub-modules (Perception, Decision, Coordination, Safety Enforcement) can be implemented as logically independent units with well-defined internal interfaces; circular dependencies between sub-modules can be identified and mitigated via Design Structure Matrix analysis.
open_risks: Telemetry validation latency: if failure detection or safe state transition exceeds allocated budget, vehicle may enter unsafe state before NGHE Solution can respond. Mitigation: establish latency requirements in next-level FR-NGHE-005.1 and verify via hardware-in-loop testing.

Decision Engine abstraction overhead: if internal abstraction layer introduces unacceptable latency, real-time performance of autonomous control may degrade. Mitigation: profile Decision Engine latency in prototype and establish maximum acceptable overhead (e.g., <10 ms).

Safety-first precedence rule enforcement: if interrupt handler pattern is not correctly implemented or tested, safety constraints may be overridden by stale teleoperation commands. Mitigation: establish explicit acceptance test in FR-NGHE-005.4 that verifies safety override behavior under simulated conflict scenarios.

Network Link message ordering: if message prioritization protocol is not enforced at network layer, safety-critical messages may be delayed or reordered. Mitigation: specify Network Link protocol in next-level DP-NGHE-005.3 and DP-NGHE-005.4 with explicit ordering guarantees; verify via network simulation.

Baseline/calibration update mechanism: if audit trail is not maintained or if performance targets are updated without proper logging, traceability to worksite safety plan changes may be lost. Mitigation: implement audit trail as mandatory feature in FR-NGHE-005.2 and establish acceptance test that verifies audit trail completeness.

Sub-module circular dependencies: if Coordination Engine and Safety Enforcement Engine have bidirectional coupling on Network Link, priority inversion or deadlock may occur under high-load conditions. Mitigation: analyze dependencies via Design Structure Matrix in next-level design and establish architectural rule that Safety Enforcement Engine is always upstream of Coordination Engine in data flow.
evidence_needed: Latency budget allocation: document maximum acceptable latency for telemetry validation failure detection ([X] ms) and safe state transition ([Y] ms); trace to vehicle control loop requirements and operator reaction time.

Decision Engine abstraction specification: document internal interface between Perception Engine and Decision Engine (data types, latency, ordering); specify abstraction layer design (e.g., state machine, event-driven, message queue) and justify latency overhead.

Safety-first precedence rule specification: document interrupt handler pattern or equivalent architectural mechanism; specify conditions under which SafetyAlert overrides coordination signals; provide pseudocode or state diagram showing precedence logic.

Network Link protocol specification: document message types, priorities, latency requirements, and ordering guarantees; specify how safety-critical messages are prioritized over coordination messages; provide protocol state machine or sequence diagram.

Baseline/calibration update mechanism specification: document how worksite safety plan changes trigger performance target recalibration; specify audit trail format (timestamp, rationale, old/new targets); define update latency requirement (e.g., within [X] hours of safety plan change).

Design Structure Matrix (DSM): document dependencies between internal sub-modules (Perception, Decision, Coordination, Safety Enforcement) and external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link); identify and mitigate circular dependencies.

Acceptance test plan for FR-NGHE-005.4: demonstrate that SafetyAlert output overrides coordination signals when conflict occurs; verify that priority inversion does not occur under simulated high-load conditions; verify that safety constraints are enforced within latency budget.

Hardware-in-loop test plan: verify telemetry validation latency and safe state transition latency under realistic worksite conditions; verify Decision Engine abstraction overhead; verify Network Link message ordering under network congestion scenarios.
